# researcher-of-agents experience — Marix

## Evidence discipline

- Pin open-source claims to commit-SHA line permalinks and re-pin fast-moving repositories before reuse. Label official, third-party, and reverse-engineered evidence separately.
- Treat unpublished provider schemas and internals as unknown. UI labels, telemetry, process launch commands, code-fence labels, and internal handler names do not establish model-facing tool identities.

## Architecture findings

- Keep persistent transcript records, provider-neutral projected context, and provider wire payloads separate. Preserve originating calls, stable call IDs, result order, and opaque provider reasoning signatures during replay.
- Treat native tool calls and assistant-text JSON as different protocols. Parse only complete successful terminal output; reject refusal, truncation, empty content, unexpected calls, and schema errors instead of extracting, repairing, or executing partial JSON.
- Approval policy, workspace restrictions, process isolation, network controls, browser-profile isolation, and VM/container sandboxing are independent guarantees; never infer one from another.

## 2026-07-20 — DeepSeek V4 thinking-mode tool choice

- **System studied:** DeepSeek V4 OpenAI-compatible Chat Completions API.
- **Primary sources:** DeepSeek [Thinking Mode](https://api-docs.deepseek.com/guides/thinking_mode/), [Create Chat Completion](https://api-docs.deepseek.com/api/create-chat-completion/), [Tool Calls](https://api-docs.deepseek.com/guides/tool_calls/), [Oh My Pi integration](https://api-docs.deepseek.com/quick_start/agent_integrations/oh_my_pi/), [model list](https://api-docs.deepseek.com/api/list-models/), and [2026-04-24 changelog](https://api-docs.deepseek.com/updates/).
- **Core modules and flow:** Direct V4 model IDs default to thinking mode; `thinking.type` toggles thinking, while `reasoning_effort` only selects effort. Tool schemas enter the model-facing request, tool calls return with `reasoning_content`, and both must be replayed across tool-result turns.
- **Reusable pattern:** Keep provider capability constraints conditional on execution mode. For DeepSeek V4 thinking mode, omit `tool_choice`; the official integration guide says the mode rejects the parameter even though the generic endpoint schema lists `none`, `auto`, and `required`. If forced tool use is a hard host contract, disable thinking before using `required`.
- **Risks / anti-patterns:** Do not infer that explicit `auto` is safe merely because omission defaults to auto when tools are present. Do not drop `reasoning_content` from assistant tool-call history. Switching from V4 Flash to V4 Pro does not remove the thinking-mode restriction.
- **Marix implication:** Represent “tool_choice parameter unsupported in thinking mode” separately from “tools unsupported.” Thinking mode supports tools, but tool selection remains model-controlled; Marix must either tolerate no tool call or use non-thinking mode when a tool call is mandatory.

## 2026-07-22 — Tool-output size caps and context-window-exceeded handling

- **Systems studied:** GitHub Copilot CLI (official, self-describing tool schemas), OpenAI Codex CLI (`openai/codex`, Rust), OpenHands SDK, Goose (Block), LangChain/LangGraph, MCP spec, Cline, browser-use, Anthropic Messages API, OpenAI Chat Completions API, LiteLLM (+ Router fallbacks), OpenCode (`anomalyco/opencode`), Aider, Cursor, Claude Code, AutoGen/AG2 (weak/inconclusive on this topic).
- **Primary sources (source-confirmed, commit- or doc-pinned):**
  - Codex CLI truncation utilities — `codex-rs/utils/string/src/truncate.rs`, `codex-rs/utils/output-truncation/src/lib.rs`, `codex-rs/protocol/src/error.rs` — github.com/openai/codex @ commit `6e5a2d6b8d148a5554fdceb6f399ca45bd1c78d9`.
  - OpenCode provider-error classifier — `packages/llm/src/provider-error.ts` — github.com/anomalyco/opencode @ commit `0a601cf334b9a83cc2854108a2b860f25e6e7e8e`.
  - GitHub Copilot CLI context management — docs.github.com/en/copilot/concepts/agents/copilot-cli/context-management (official).
  - Anthropic `stop_reason` reference — platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/handling-stop-reasons (official).
  - LiteLLM exception mapping and fallback routing — docs.litellm.ai/docs/exception_mapping, docs.litellm.ai/docs/proxy/reliability (official).
  - OpenHands condenser architecture — docs.openhands.dev/sdk/arch/condenser (official).
  - Goose smart context management — goose-docs.ai/docs/guides/sessions/smart-context-management (official).
  - MCP pagination scope (list operations only, not tool-result content) — official spec, confirmed same session.
  - Secondary/community only (not maintainer-confirmed): Cline issues #4576/#4389, browser-use issue #5126, Aider's reactive-only behavior, Cursor/Claude Code auto-summarize claims.
- **Core modules and flow:** Two independent failure modes, both present in Marix's own incident. (1) A single oversized tool result entering a prompt/history builder with no cap. (2) A provider signaling mid- or pre-generation that the assembled request/response overflowed context, via a value the client's finish-reason parser doesn't recognize. Mature systems address each with a different module: a **truncation/formatting layer** sitting between tool execution and prompt assembly (Codex CLI's `TruncationPolicy` + shared middle-truncation primitive; Copilot CLI's 20 KiB-to-tempfile spill), and a **provider-response classifier** sitting between the raw API response and the agent's control flow (OpenCode's regex-based `isContextOverflow`/`classification: "context-overflow"` tagging; LiteLLM's typed `ContextWindowExceededError`).
- **Reusable pattern (Q1 — tool-output size):** Prefer **one shared, reusable truncation primitive invoked with different per-call-site budgets**, not a single global constant and not fully bespoke per-tool logic. Codex CLI is the cleanest confirmed example: one middle-truncation function, ~8 distinct named budget constants at different call sites (rejection messages, exec output, memory summaries, additional-context values, etc.), and the *same* primitive reused for whole-history compaction, not just per-item truncation. When truncating, keep head+tail (not just head or just tail) on character boundaries, and prepend a marker that states the *original* size (byte/token count and/or line count) so the model knows how much was hidden. Reserve LLM-based summarization for whole-history compaction triggered by a size/count threshold, not as the first response to one oversized tool result — that role is filled by cheap synchronous truncation in every system studied. Pagination via a re-callable cursor/offset param in the tool's own schema (Copilot CLI's `web_fetch(start_index)`/`view(view_range)`) is real but is a tool-schema-level convention, not something the MCP protocol itself provides for tool-call results (MCP pagination is list-operation-only).
- **Reusable pattern (Q2 — context-window-exceeded handling):** Providers signal overflow in incompatible shapes — OpenAI rejects pre-generation via HTTP 400 + `error.code == "context_length_exceeded"`; Anthropic returns a normal HTTP 200 with `stop_reason == "model_context_window_exceeded"` and says to treat the response as truncated; GLM's OpenAI-compatible shim leaks Anthropic's string value into an OpenAI-shaped `finish_reason` field, matching neither spec exactly. Robust clients therefore use **two detection layers together**: exact enum/exception-type matching for well-integrated providers, plus a defensive fuzzy pattern-matching classifier (with an exclusion list to avoid false-positiving on rate-limit messages) for non-standard ones — OpenCode's `isContextOverflow` is a direct, near-identical precedent that already recognizes the exact string that broke Marix. Once detected, the mature systems normalize the raw signal into **one typed/tagged internal condition**, decoupled from the original wire format, before any recovery logic runs. Recovery is best modeled as a **hybrid**, explicitly documented as such by Goose ("two-tiered... layered approach") and OpenHands (Automatic vs. Manual condenser triggers): a proactive threshold-based check runs on every step *before* the provider ever complains, with a reactive fallback (typed error + optional single retry with trimmed/summarized history) only for the residual case where the provider still overflows anyway. I found no solid precedent for reactively targeting the single *largest* historical blob after a provider error — real systems avoid that need by capping item size proactively at insertion time instead (see Q1 pattern), and their reactive fallback is age-priority (drop/summarize oldest), not size-priority.
- **Risks / anti-patterns:** A strict finish-reason allow-list (accept only exact known-good values, hard-fail on everything else) breaks the moment any provider's OpenAI-compatible shim leaks a non-standard value — this is precisely Marix's incident and is also a documented, previously-hit bug in OpenCode against the same GLM/Zhipu behavior. Naive oldest-message-dropping with no record (Cline's fallback) is explicitly called out by GitHub Copilot CLI's own docs as the inferior alternative compaction is designed to avoid. Hard-blocking oversized tool output outright (Cline's >300 KB file-read block) rather than truncating gracefully is a real, citable anti-pattern, not a hypothetical.
- **Marix implication:** For the `append_tool_calls` unbounded-concatenation bug, add one shared truncation helper (Codex-CLI-style: middle-truncate, character-boundary-safe, quantified marker) and apply it at the point tool output enters `prompt.rs`'s concatenation functions, with a smaller budget for inherently huge/low-density sources like `web_fetch` than for others. For the `parse_finish_reason` hard-fail bug, replace the `"stop"|"tool_calls"`-only allow-list with a small, extensible recognized-overflow-value set (starting with `"model_context_window_exceeded"`) that maps to a new distinguishable `ModelBackendError::ContextWindowExceeded` variant instead of the generic `InvalidResponse`, mirroring OpenCode's typed-classification approach; have the relay layer catch that variant and retry once with the Q1 truncation helper applied more aggressively, rather than failing the task outright. Treat this as a minimal reactive safety net; a proactive token-estimation pre-check (Goose/Copilot-CLI-style ~80% threshold) is a reasonable later enhancement, not required to close this specific incident.

## 2026-07-23 — web_fetch/web_search pagination and caching conventions across agent CLIs

- **Systems studied:** MCP reference `fetch` server (`modelcontextprotocol/servers`) — read directly, twice, independently; GitHub Copilot CLI's own `web_fetch` (self-introspected schema only, no backend visibility); Anthropic Claude Code CLI `WebFetch`/`WebSearch` + Anthropic API `web_fetch`/`web_search` server tools (official docs + community reverse-engineering, confidence labeled per-claim); Cline (`cline/cline`); Continue (`continuedev/continue`); OpenHands legacy (`All-Hands-AI/OpenHands` tag 0.39.0) and current SDK (`OpenHands/software-agent-sdk`); Goose (`aaif-goose/goose`, formerly `block/goose`); Cursor (official docs + leaked schema); Windsurf/Cascade (official docs + two generations of leaked schema); Aider (`Aider-AI/aider`); OpenAI Codex CLI (`openai/codex`); MCP brave-search (archived), Tavily MCP, Exa MCP, Perplexity MCP as web-search comparators.
- **Primary sources (source-confirmed, file/line- or doc-pinned):**
  - MCP fetch server — `modelcontextprotocol/servers:src/fetch/src/mcp_server_fetch/server.py` (blob SHA `b42c7b1f6b7ce1fea6b50a5a4554f3436cb36131`) — read in full directly.
  - Codex CLI web-search action protocol — `openai/codex:codex-rs/protocol/src/models.rs` (`WebSearchAction`/`WebSearchCall`, schemars-renamed `ResponsesApiWebSearchAction`), `codex-rs/core/src/web_search.rs`, `codex-rs/analytics/src/events.rs` (`WebSearchActionKind`) — read directly, commit `66bd101fff6f0e7e05a594ec7bdb78b92f6b66d3`. Zero hits for any `web_fetch`/`webfetch` symbol anywhere in the repo (searched directly).
  - Anthropic official docs: `platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/web-fetch-tool`, `.../web-search-tool`, `platform.claude.com/docs/en/release-notes/overview` (via sub-agent web_fetch/web_search, HIGH confidence, verbatim schema quotes).
  - Cline — `cline/cline:sdk/packages/core/src/extensions/tools/schemas.ts`, `.../executors/web-fetch.ts`, `sdk/examples/plugins/web-search.ts` (all read directly by sub-agent, file SHAs recorded in-session).
  - Continue — `continuedev/continue:core/tools/definitions/fetchUrlContent.ts`, `.../searchWeb.ts`, `core/tools/implementations/{fetchUrlContent,searchWeb}.ts`, `core/context/providers/{URLContextProvider,WebContextProvider}.ts` (read directly).
  - OpenHands — legacy `openhands/agenthub/codeact_agent/tools/web_read.py` (tag 0.39.0); current `OpenHands/software-agent-sdk:openhands-tools/openhands/tools/browser_use/{definition.py,server.py}`, `openhands-sdk/openhands/sdk/utils/truncate.py` (read directly).
  - Goose — `aaif-goose/goose:crates/goose-mcp/src/computercontroller/mod.rs` (read directly).
  - Cursor — `cursor.com/docs/agent/overview.md` (official, thin); `x1xhlol/system-prompts-and-models-of-ai-tools:Cursor Prompts/Agent Tools v1.0.json` (leaked, explicitly labeled unofficial).
  - Windsurf — `docs.devin.ai/desktop/cascade/web-search` (official, post-Cognition-acquisition doc host); `elder-plinius/CL4R1T4S/WINDSURF/Windsurf_Tools.md`, `jujumilk3/leaked-system-prompts/codeium-windsurf-*`, `x1xhlol/system-prompts-and-models-of-ai-tools/Windsurf/Tools Wave 11.txt` (leaked, two schema generations diffed).
  - Aider — `Aider-AI/aider:aider/commands.py` (`cmd_web`), `aider/scrape.py` (`Scraper`) (read directly).
  - MCP brave-search (archived) — `modelcontextprotocol/servers-archived:src/brave-search/index.ts`; Brave official API docs `api-dashboard.search.brave.com/api-reference/web/search/get`. Tavily — `tavily-ai/tavily-mcp:src/index.ts`. Exa — `exa-labs/exa-mcp-server:src/tools/{webSearch,webSearchAdvanced}.ts`, `src/types.ts`. Perplexity — `perplexityai/modelcontextprotocol:src/server.ts`. All read directly.
- **Core modules and flow:** Every system separates into the same two tool families (fetch-a-URL vs. search-the-web), but they diverge sharply on whether "get more" is a client-visible, re-callable operation at all. Three fetch-pagination shapes were found in the wild, all keyed differently: (1) **offset+length** (`start_index`+`max_length` — MCP reference server, and by extension this environment's own `web_fetch`; OpenHands SDK's `start_from_char`) where the *server* computes and hands back the exact next offset in the response text; (2) **page-number+opaque-id** (Windsurf's `view_web_document_content_chunk(url, position)` evolving to `view_content_chunk(document_id, position)` — a two-call protocol: read once, then page by integer `position` against either the raw URL or a provider-issued ID); (3) **no client-visible pagination at all** — the fetch either returns a provider/model-summarized answer with no raw offset (Claude Code CLI's `WebFetch(url, prompt)` → Haiku-summarized), or a hard single-shot truncation with a plain-text warning and no continuation path (Cline, Continue, Goose, Aider). A fourth, structurally distinct shape showed up only in the two frontier-lab-hosted tools: **provider-server-side delegation** — OpenAI's Responses API `web_search` hosted tool internally sequences `Search`→`OpenPage`→`FindInPage` (the last being a literal grep-with-pattern-in-a-specific-page action), and Anthropic's `web_fetch`/`web_search` server tools run entirely on Anthropic's infrastructure; in both cases the *client* CLI (Codex CLI, Claude Code) never constructs or sees pagination parameters — it only parses/replays a stream of provider-driven action events for display and history purposes. This is a different axis from (1)-(3): the pagination/search-refinement loop moves off the client entirely onto the model provider's own servers.
- **Reusable pattern (fetch caching — the central question):** Every *client-side* fetch tool inspected (MCP reference server — verified twice by direct code read; Cline; Continue; OpenHands new SDK's `_get_content`; Goose) **re-fetches from the network and re-runs HTML→text conversion on every single call, including every paginated continuation call**, then reslices/reprocesses from scratch — there is no `{url → content}` memoization anywhere in any of these five codebases. The only *content-level* cache found was Claude Code CLI's WebFetch (reverse-engineered only, MEDIUM confidence: 15-minute in-process LRU, 50 MB cap, keyed by literal URL string, exposed via a `clearWebFetchCache()` function) and Anthropic's API `web_fetch` server tool (officially documented to cache, explicit `use_cache: false` bypass flag exists, but Anthropic deliberately does not document TTL, scope, or key mechanism — "managed automatically and may change over time"). Two systems cache something *adjacent* to content rather than content itself: OpenHands (legacy BrowserGym subprocess and new-SDK singleton Playwright/Chromium instance persist as a live **browser session** across calls — cookies/DOM/navigation state persist, but each `web_read`/`browser_get_content` call still re-extracts text from the live DOM fresh) and Goose (`web_scrape` writes every fetch to a **new timestamped file** in a cache directory — this is "cache" only in the sense of a saved-artifact store browsable later via a separate `cache` tool; it is never consulted to avoid a re-fetch, and the same URL fetched twice always produces two files and two network calls). Windsurf's leaked schema is the one case implying a true fetch-once-serve-many-chunks cache (`view_..._chunk` explicitly requires the URL/document to have been `read_url_content`'d first), but this is reverse-engineered (no response-shape or TTL confirmed) and its own docs describe the reading as chunked/selective, not necessarily "fetch everything once, chunk client-side."
- **Reusable pattern (truncation signaling):** The most robust, verified pattern is the MCP reference server's: embed the *exact next parameter value* the model should pass, directly in the truncated text body (`"Content truncated. Call the fetch tool with a start_index of {next_start} to get more content."`), computed server-side so the model does no arithmetic — but notably this message does **not** include total content length, only the next offset (so the model cannot know in advance how many more calls it will take). OpenHands SDK's message is nearly identical in spirit (`"... (truncated, use start_from_char={next} to continue)"`) plus a second, independent, generic `maybe_truncate` layer (50,000-char `DEFAULT_TEXT_CONTENT_LIMIT`) that also persists the untruncated content to a save-dir file and reports the file path — a hybrid of "give a continuation parameter" and "give an escape hatch to the full content via filesystem" in the same system. Systems with no pagination path (Cline, Continue, Goose, Aider, Cursor) still uniformly signal truncation via a plain-text/warning message naming the limit, but explicitly tell the model to *change strategy* (narrower query, more targeted fetch) rather than continue — e.g. Continue's exact wording: `"...consider fetching specific sections or using a more targeted approach."` No system studied returns a structured boolean/numeric field (`truncated: bool`, `has_more`, `total_length`) in the *tool result schema* itself — every truncation signal found, across all ~10 systems, is communicated as an appended natural-language sentence inside the text content, not a separate typed field.
- **Reusable pattern (search pagination):** Only Brave's API (traditional inverted-index search) has real offset-based paging exposed through its MCP wrapper (`count` 1-20 default 10, `offset` 0-9 as a *page index*, not a result index — confirmed against Brave's own docs; max reachable results = count×10 = 200). Every semantic/neural search API surveyed (Tavily, Exa, Perplexity, Anthropic's and OpenAI's hosted web_search) has **no offset/page/cursor parameter at all** — only a flat `max_results`/`numResults` cap (typically default 5-10, max 20-100) — because their retrieval isn't a stable paged index; the documented workaround everywhere is "issue a new, more specific/refined query," never "page 2." The one universally-converged-upon feature among the modern semantic-search wrappers is a **search+fetch-in-one-call shortcut**: Tavily's `include_raw_content` boolean and Exa's `contents: {text/highlights/summary}` object both let full (or near-full) page content ride back in the *same* search response, avoiding a second fetch round-trip entirely — this is a materially different, and more efficient, chaining design than Anthropic's/OpenAI's "search returns links, then a separate fetch tool call is needed" two-step pattern. Exa additionally exposes cache-freshness as a caller-visible knob (`maxAgeHours`, `livecrawl: "fallback"|"preferred"`), i.e. it admits server-side caching exists and lets the caller trade staleness for speed — no other system studied exposes this dial.
- **Risks / anti-patterns:** (1) Hard single-shot truncation with a "try a different approach" message and no continuation path (Cline/Continue/Goose/Aider) forces the model to burn a full extra turn guessing a narrower query instead of cheaply reading on — acceptable only if page sizes are rarely exceeded in practice. (2) Re-fetch-and-reslice-from-scratch on every paginated call (the majority pattern, including the MCP reference server this environment's own `web_fetch` schema descends from) means content can drift mid-pagination if the remote page changes between calls, and wastes full network+HTML-parse cost per page even though only a cache-populate-once operation is logically needed — confirmed by direct code reading, not inferred. (3) Not exposing total content length anywhere (MCP server, OpenHands) leaves the model unable to plan how many follow-up calls a full read will take, only whether one more call is needed. (4) Windsurf's `document_id`-keyed v2 schema is evidence that URL-keyed continuation calls can be deliberately hardened into opaque-ID-keyed ones — a direction worth noting even though it wasn't verifiable at the response-schema level.
- **Marix implication:** For `web_fetch`, the field-name/shape choice already matches the most common convention (`start_index`+`max_length`, offset/length style) and needs no redesign there; the two gaps worth closing are (a) no system studied justifies skipping the "tell the model the exact next start_index in the truncation message" step — Marix should adopt this exact pattern rather than only setting a `truncated` boolean, since no real system relies on a structured flag alone; (b) caching is genuinely rare and inconsistent even among mature systems (only two credible examples, one MEDIUM-confidence/reverse-engineered, one officially vague), so a from-scratch reslice-on-refetch design is not an anti-pattern by industry norms — it is in fact the majority default — but *if* Marix adds a cache it should be justified primarily by the concrete incident cost (849 KB page re-parsed repeatedly) rather than by "everyone does this," since most don't. For `web_search`, Marix's flat `max_results` (1-10) with no pagination matches the modern semantic-search-tool majority (Tavily/Exa/Perplexity/Anthropic/OpenAI all lack true pagination too) — the higher-value gap is not adding page/offset support but adding a Tavily/Exa-style "include full content of top result(s) inline" option to cut the common search→fetch two-call sequence to one call.
